The five daughters of Zelophehad felt they were left without support, having neither father nor brother to inherit land. Their confident expectation that the Lord’s word would be fulfilled in due time, and their desire for a share in the promised inheritance, along with the modest and open way they asked, without secret complaints or unhappiness, are good examples. They requested ownership of land in Canaan. In this request, they showed 1. Strong faith in God's power and promise regarding giving the land of Canaan to Israel. 2. And a sincere desire for a place and name in the promised land, which represented heaven. 3. Respect and honor for their father, whose name was important to them now that he was gone. He had never done anything that would prevent his children from claiming what was rightfully theirs. It is comforting to parents when they die if, though they have suffered for their own sins, they are not conscious of any iniquities that God will punish in their children. God himself gives judgment. He notices the affairs, not only of nations but also of private families, and arranges them according to his will. The petition is granted. Those who seek an inheritance in the land of promise will receive what they seek, and other blessings will be added to them.

Moses must die, but he will have the satisfaction of seeing the land of promise. This sight of Canaan signified his confident expectation of a better place, that is, heaven. Moses must die, but death does not cut him off; it only brings him to rest with the holy patriarchs. It is simply dying as they died, having lived as they lived; and since their end was peace, why should we fear any evil in passing through that dark valley?

Jealous spirits do not love their successors, but Moses was not one of these. We should be concerned, both in our prayers and efforts, for the rising generation, so that religion may be maintained and advanced when we are gone. God appoints a successor, even Joshua; who distinguished himself by his courage in fighting Amalek, his humility in serving Moses, and his faith and honesty in opposing the report of the evil spies. God appoints this man to succeed Moses; a man in whom is the Spirit, the Spirit of grace. He is a good man, fearing God and hating greed, and acting from principle. He has the spirit of leadership; he is fit to do the work and fulfill the responsibilities of his position. He has a spirit of conduct and courage; he also had the Spirit of prophecy. That person is not fully qualified for any service in the church of Christ who lacks the graces and gifts of the Holy Spirit, whatever human abilities they may possess. And in Joshua’s succession we are reminded “that the law was given by Moses,” who because of our transgression could not bring us to heaven; but “grace and truth came by Jesus Christ,” for the salvation of every believer.
